coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via a facile reflux assisted polymerization of N-Methyl-Pyrrolidone for hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yang
Ye, Nini
Li, Xingqiu
Li, Xian
Liu, Han
Wang, Enze
Liang, Chenglu
Peng, Xiangfang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have shown great potential in catalysis for energy conversion. In this work, a facile and one-pot reflux assisted polymerization of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) was developed to synthesize the N-CQDs with large scales. The as-obtained N-CQDs possess a high N/C molar ratio of 46% and the doped Nitrogen existed mainly as pyrrolic N. More interesting, the N-CQDs exhibited satisfying catalytic activity towards hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) due to the existed pyrrolic N. Compared with other synthesis methods, our method is more convenient and can be amplified under atmospheric pressure, which showed great practical application significance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
N-Methyl pyrrolidone
Hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photographs of N-CQDs synthesized at 240 ​°C for different time under (a) natural light and (b) ultraviolet light.

(a) Photographs of blank solvent of pristine NMP and N-CQDs-17h under ultraviolet. (b) PL emission spectra of NMP refluxed at different temperatures for 17h using 421 ​nm excitation wavelength. (c) UV–Vis absorption of N-CQDs. (d) PL emission spectra of the N-CQDs refluxed at 240 ​°C for different time using 421 ​nm excitation wavelength.
(a) Polarization curves, (b) the Tafel plots and (c) Nyquist plots of N-CQDs catalysts loaded on a glassy carbon electrode. (d) Stability tests for the N-CQDs catalyst for 4000 cycles.

Electrochemical double layer capacitance curves of (a)N-CQDs-1h, (b)N-CQDs-5h, (c)N-CQDs-10h, (d)N-CQDs-17h and (e)N-CQDs-24h with different scan rates from 5 ​mV ​s−1 to 400 mV ​s−1 in 0.5 ​M ​H2SO4. Plots of Cdl (f) and specific activities of samples.
The TEM images of N-CQDs after different reflux durations. (a) (b) (c)N-CQDs-1h, (d) (e) (f)N-CQDs-10h, (j) (k) (i)N-CQDs-24h.
(a) The AFM image and (b) TEM image of N-CQDs-17 ​h ​at low amplification; (c) The HRTEM image and (d) corresponding particle size distribution histograms of N-CQDs-17h.
(a) X-ray photoelectron survey spectrum of N-CQDs-17h. The high resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s signal of N-CQDs-17h. (d) Raman spectrum of N-CQDs-17h.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots via a facile reflux assisted polymerization of N-Methyl-Pyrrolidone for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, Fuzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, Fuzhou, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have shown great potential in catalysis for energy conversion. In this work, a facile and one-pot reflux assisted polymerization of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) was developed to synthesize the N-CQDs with large scales. The as-obtained N-CQDs possess a high N/C molar ratio of 46% and the doped Nitrogen existed mainly as pyrrolic N. More interesting, the N-CQDs exhibited satisfying catalytic activity towards hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) due to the existed pyrrolic N. Compared with other synthesis methods, our method is more convenient and can be amplified under atmospheric pressure, which showed great practical application significance.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were successfully synthesized under atmospheric pressure by a facile and one-pot reflux assisted polymerization of N-Methyl-Pyrrolidone (NMP) and displayed a satisfying performance towards electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, hydrogen energy has proven its potential as an ideal and environment-friendly energy storage alternative towards the shortage of oil resources. Water splitting is the main clean-energy technology for hydrogen generation. However, the practical applications of water splitting are very limited because the cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) requires large over potentials [1,2]. Achieving HER in an efficient and cost-effective way is crucial for water splitting industry. The design of appropriate electrocatalysts is the key to realize efficient electrocatalytic HER. Pt-based nanomaterials with negligible overpotentials for conducting HER are state-of-the-art catalysts [3]. Unfortunately, the exorbitant cost and limited supply have eliminated the large-scale application in energy conversion.

Carbon based materials have been discovered to be a promising alternative to noble metal catalysts for the HER [4]. Over the decades, this new catalyst developed rapidly. A series of carbon based materials with excellent catalytic properties, including carbon nanotubes/fibers [5], graphene [6], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [7]were developed. Due to the size effects, CQDs have long been thought to have very distinctive chemical and physical properties compared with macroscopic carbon. The heteroatom doped CQDs with N [8], S [9], P [10], B [11] and other non-metallic elements or metal elements [7,12–14] have been developed. Most recently, CQDs and heteroatom doped CQDs are highlighted in terms of high specific surface area, abundant edge defects and high surface activity, thus rendering them greatly attractive in electrocatalysis [15–20]. The CQDs have been reported to have an inherent reducing property and be able to reduce metal ions to the corresponding zerovalent metal nanoparticles, and thus forming metal/CQDs composite catalyst [21]. CQDs were usually used as a nonmetallic promoter of metal catalyst to enhance the HER activities [22]. The electrochemical HER performance of pristine CQDs in metal/CQDs composite catalysts was ignored and seldom reported. Herein, the electrochemical HER performance of N-doped CQDs was investigated.

Doping CQDs with nitrogen generally redistributes charge density and spin density of carbon atoms, thus effectively regulating the surface properties of CQDs and increasing adsorption of reactants at active sites [23–26]. N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were usually synthesized using molecular precursors as starting materials [27–29]. The main approaches include hydrothermal method [30,31], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [32] and microwave assisted method [27,33,34]. However, these method requires complex instruments. Therefore, exploration of facile preparation method for N-CQDs under relatively moderate conditions and convenient method is desired.

Herein, a facile and one-pot reflux assisted polymerization of N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) was developed to synthesize the N-CQDs with large scales in this work. The as-obtained N-CQDs possess a high N/C molar ratio of 46% and the doped Nitrogen existed mainly as pyrrolic N. The lattice spacing in the N-CQDs is close to the typical (100) diffraction plane of graphite according to the high resolution transmission electron microscope observation. The particle size of N-CQDS is uniformly distributed with an average diameter of 3.0 ​nm. N-CQDs could be formed at a temperature greater than 100 ​°C. When treated at 300 ​°C, the CQDs were formed even after 1 ​h reflux assisted polymerization, and with the increase of reflux time to 17h, the N-CQDs showed more uniform shape and size distribution and the performance of HER maximized. When the reflux time is over 17h, excessive treatment showed an adverse influence on the generation of well-defined N-CQDs, causing a crosslinking of the N-CQDs to form bigger carbon spheres. More interesting, the N-CQDs exhibited satisfying catalytic activities towards hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) due to the existed pyrrolic N. Compared with other synthesis methods, our method is more convenient and can be amplified under atmospheric pressure, which showed great practical application significance.

The reagents used were analytically pure and used without further purification. 100 ​ml NMP and 1g zeolite were added to the 250 ​mL three-necked flask equipped with the reflux setup. The products obtained by reflux treating NMP at 50 ​°C, 100 ​°C, 200 ​°C and 240 ​°C for 17h were named NMP-50, NMP-100, NMP-200 and NMP-240 respectively. The sample treated at 240 ​°C for 1, 5, 10, 17 and 24 ​h were named as N-CQDs-1h, N-CQDs-5h, N-CQDs-10h, N-CQDs-17h and N-CQDs-24h respectively. The suspension obtained after reflux was subjected to dialysis (10000 ​Da dialysis membrane) for 3 days.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained using JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope. The normal TEM samples were prepared by dropping the solution onto a copper grid covered with a carbon film and drying in air. Raman spectra were collected on the hr800 Raman spectrometer (J Y, France) equipped with a synapse CCD detector and an Olympus confocal microscope. The spectrometer used a 600 ​g ​mm−1 grating and a 633 ​nm He-Ne laser. UV absorption at room temperature was recorded on a UV2600 spectrophotometer with a wavelength range of 200–900 ​nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted by using a KRATOS Axis Ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer into a monochromatic Al K radiation (h ​= ​1486.6 ​eV). Atomic force microscope (AFM) samples were prepared by drip-drying the aqueous solution of the sample on a silicon substrate. PL was studied on FLS980 luminescent spectrometer using 421 ​nm excitation wavelength.

Electrocatalytic measurements were performed using an electrochemical workstation (AT302 ​N, AUTOLAB, Switzerland) in a common three-electrode electrochemical cell. The working electrode is made of vitrified carbon (geometric area ​= ​0.0707 ​cm−2) with catalyst. Saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum wire were used as reference electrode and counter electrode respectively. To prepare the working electrode, the sample suspensions (15 ​μL) were dropped onto the glass carbon electrode followed by coating with 5 ​μL Nafion solution (0.5 ​wt %). Finally, the treated glassy carbon electrode was dried at room temperature. The electrolyte was 0.5 ​M ​H2SO4 aqueous solution. The potentials were converted into values referred to reversible hydrogen electrode, using the equation of Evs RHE ​= ​Evs SCE ​+ ​Eθ SCE + 0.059 pH, the electrocatalytic activities of Pt/C (10 ​wt%) were measured under similar conditions. 10 ​mg ​Pt/C was ultrasonically dispersed in 2 ​mL water with 100 ​μl 5% Nafion to form the catalyst ink and about 15 ​μL catalyst ink was coated on the glassy carbon electrode. The polarization curve was measured by linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) and the scanning rate was 5 ​mV ​s−1. The electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs) were determined by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The potential of CV in 0.5 ​M ​H2SO4 was 0 ​V (versus RHE). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were conducted by AC impedance with an amplitude of 0.399 ​V under open circuit potential from 1000 ​kHz to 10 ​mHz.

The photographs of N-CQDs suspensions synthesized for different time under natural light (Fig. 1 a) and ultraviolet light(Fig. 1b) were shown. And obvious differences can be observed by naked eye. As the time of reflux treatment increased, the color of suspensions for N-CQDs deepened under the daylight. In addition, the N-CQDs emitted intense blue luminescence under irradiation by a 375 ​nm UV lamp. The N-CQDs-17h showed the strongest photoluminescence (PL) effect under UV light.

It is worth mentioning that the blank solvent displayed no obvious PL effect under UV light as compared with N-CQDs-17h (Fig. 2 a). The optical properties of the samples were investigated by UV visible absorption and PL spectroscopy. The PL emission spectra of samples obtained by reflux at different temperatures for 17h were shown in Fig. 2b. The pristine NMP and NMP-50 exhibited no obvious emission peaks, which indicated that CQDs were not formed in the suspensions. While the NMP-100, NMP-200 and NMP-240 all displayed significant emission peaks at about 500 ​nm, ascribed to the formation of CQDs. The intensity of emission peak of NMP-240 was the strongest, indicating a high content of CQDs. UV–vis spectra (Fig. 2c) of the N-CQDs synthesized for different time at 240 ​°C showed a broad absorption band in the 250–350 ​nm region with a tail extending to the visible light region, characteristic of a polycyclic aromatic π system [35]. Specifically, the peaks in all N-CQDs at 270–300 ​nm were ascribed to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic domains in the carbonized core of the N-CQDs. The absorption peaks for π–π∗ transition gradually red-shifted as the heating time increased, probably due to the conjugated effects derived from the auxochrome groups of –OH, –OR or –NH2 gradually formed on the N-CQDs surfaces [24,36,37]. Moreover, the N-CQDs-17h and N-CQDs-24h displayed an absorption edge at approximately 350 ​nm, which can be attributed to the n-π∗ transition of surface functional groups, such as C=O/C–N [38–41].

The PL emission spectra (Fig. 2d) of the N-CQDs synthesized for different time at 240 ​°C showed a noticeable emission peak centered at about 500 ​nm, except for N-CQDs-1h. Through further observation of the emission peaks, a slight red shift followed by a blue shift was observed in the PL spectra. Similar blue-shifts in the PL emission band of N-CQDs have been reported by Li et al. [42]and Zhang et al. [43]. Mondal and Saha [44] proposed that with increasing content of nitrogen in carbon dots, nitrogen related defect states increased near conduction band of carbon quantum dots and resulted in red shift of emission peaks. The slight red shift in N-CQDs treated for 5h, 10h and 17h were probably due to the increased content of nitrogen in the CQDs, while the followed blue shift in N-CQDs-24h was due to the decreased nitrogen content after excessive reflux duration.

The HER performances of N-CQDs catalysts were tested and the polarization curves of the prepared N-CQDs obtained at 240 ​°C under different reflux time (1, 5, 10, 17 and 24 ​h) were presented in Fig. 3 a. The potential value at the current density of 10 ​mA ​cm−2 was an important criterion frequently used to estimate the electrochemical catalytic properties of catalysts. The N-CQDs-17h only required an overpotential (η) of 341 ​mV to deliver a 10 ​mA ​cm−2 current density, comparable with commercial Pt/C (91.6 ​mV to deliver a 10 ​mA ​cm−2 current density). While the overpotentials were about 522 ​mV for N-CQDs-1h, N-CQDs-5h, N-CQDs-10h, N-CQDs-24h to deliver a current density of 10 ​mA ​cm−2. The Tafel plots yielded from overpotentials versus log current densities were summarized to further illustrate the fluent charge transport. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Tafel slope of N-CQDs-17h was merely 126 ​mV per decade, which was much lower than that of N-CQDs-1h (187 ​mV/decade), N-CQDs-5h (200 mV/decade), N-CQDs-10h (185 mV/decade) and N-CQDs-24h (182 mV/decade), indicating a much better charge transfer rate in N-CQDs-17h. To study the electrode dynamics under catalytic conditions of HER, the electrochemical impedance spectra of N-CQDs refluxed at 240 ​°C for different time were analyzed. The Nyquist curves in Fig. 3c showed that N-CQDs-17h displayed much lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than others. The relatively low Rct of N-CQDs-17h suggested satisfying electron-transfer ability within electrode, which may result in better HER performances. Fig. 3d exhibited the polarization curves of N-CQDs before and after 4000 cycles. The N-CQDs catalyst showed a negligible loss in catalytic activity with slight current deterioration, indicating an excellent electrochemical durability.

The electrochemical active surface areas (ECSAs) was used to characterize the density of electrochemical active site for materials. Double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was calculated by CV measurements. The CV curves of N-CQDs-1h, N-CQDs-5h, N-CQDs-10h, N-CQDs-17h and N-CQDs-24h were shown in Fig. 4 a-e. Double-layer charging currents were plotted with scan rates as presented in Fig. 4f. The double-layer capacitance was calculated from the slope, and the values were 6.1, 7.2, 90.6, 160.7 and 93.6 ​μF ​cm−2 for N-CQDs-1h, N-CQDs-5h, N-CQDs-10h, N-CQDs-17h and N-CQDs-24h, respectively. The higher Cdl values of N-CQDs-17h indicated a higher ECSA [45,46]. That is to say, the N-CQDs-17h possessed the largest surface active sites, which in turn endowed the catalyst with the best HER performances.

The TEM images of the as-obtained N-CQDs were shown in Fig. 5 . According to the TEM results, N-CQDs were formed even after a treatment for 1h. With the increase in the heating time, N-CQDs showed a more uniform shape and size distribution (N-CQDs-17h). The excessive heating treatment has an adverse influence on the generation of well-defined N-CQDs, causing a crosslinking of the N-CQDs to form bigger carbon spheres (N-CQDs-24h).

The CQDs-17h were further characterized by AFM and TEM (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6a, the distribution of carbon quantum dots was uniform and the particle size was small. Fig. 6b shows the image of the N-CQDs, revealing that they are spherical and uniform nanoparticles. The HRTEM images (Fig. 6c) showed that nanoparticles were crystallized. The lattice spacing of 0.22 ​nm was very close to the typical (100) diffraction plane of graphite, which indicated the formation of a graphitic core within the as-obtained N-CQDs [36]. The corresponding particle size distribution histograms (Fig. 6d) showed a relatively narrow size distribution (2.5–3.55 ​nm) of the N-CQDs with an average diameter of 3.0 ​nm.

XPS measurements were carried out to establish the elemental composition of N-CQDs-17h. The survey scan spectrum indicated a pronounced N1s peak at 399.5 ​eV, a predominant C1s peak at 284.5 ​eV, and an O1s peak at 531.5 ​eV (Fig. 7 a), which indeed confirmed the successful formation of N-CQDs. The N/C atomic ratio was calculated to be 46%, which is higher than that of previously reported N-CQDs [31,47,48]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 7b) of N-CQDs further confirmed the presence of C–C/C=C (284.8 ​eV), C–O/C-N (286.2 ​eV) and C=O (287.9 ​eV) structures. In addition, the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 7c) of N-CQDs-17h revealed the presence of pyrrolic (399.5 ​eV). As shown in Fig. 7d, a D-band (sp3-hybridized) at 1346 ​cm−1 and a G-band (sp2-hybridized) at 1592 ​cm−1 were observed in the Raman spectrum, which were characteristic for carbon based materials [13]. The peak intensity ratio of D band to G band (I D/I G) is normally used to measure the defect amount of the materials. The I D/I G of N-CQDs is 0.56, which is lower than the previous literature [9,49,50]. The considerably low ID/IG signified the graphitic sp2-bonded C=C structures for both pristine CQDs and N-CQDs [49].

Recently, nitrogen-doped carbon materials have become a type of promising electrocatalysts [51,52]. Among these catalysts, doped N species including pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N were thought to be the active sites which enhanced significantly the electrocatalytic activity of materials [53,54]. Nitrogen doping in the form of pyridinic-N was mostly reported [51,52,55–57]. While in this work, the CQDs was doped solely in the form of pyrrole-N. The comparison of N-CQDs in this work with other reported nitrogen-doped carbon materials toward HER were summarized in Table S1. The synthesized N-CQDs in this work displayed competitive HER catalytic activities with a relatively low overpotential.

It is inferred that the remarkable catalytic performance and charge transfer mechanism in N-CQDs for the HER can be mainly attributed to the following synergistic effects. The doping of N atoms in carbon matrix can regulate the electronic properties of adjacent carbon atoms through intramolecular charge transfer, which is beneficial for the electrocatalytic performance. In addition, the doped nitrogen atoms in N-CQDs also participate in the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution process in the form of pyrrole-N as the active site. The pyrrolic N are more electron withdrawing than graphitic N, facilitating the adsorption of H2O molecules (△EH2O) by offsetting the energy level match of H2O. The pyrrolic-N has a lower energy barrier for the initial H2O dissociation (△GH2O), facilitating the step in which adsorbed H2O generates a proton (Volmer step). Meanwhile, The |△GH∗| value of pyrrole-N doped with multiple N atoms is smaller, indicating a balance between the adsorption and desorption processes for enhancing the subsequent hydrogen evolution step (Heyrovsky step). Much of pyrrole nitrogen doped lead to low |△GH∗| with high electron transfer rate of carbon quantum dots interaction led to the emergence of the phenomenon of high electric catalytic activity of hydrogen evolution [26,58–61].

A facile reflux assisted method at atmospheric pressure was developed to prepare N-CQDs in a large scale using NMP as a single feedstock. The N-CQDs with a size about 3 ​nm possessed a graphitic core and a high C/N atomic ratio of 46%, mainly existed as pyrrolic N. More interesting, the N-CQDs exhibited satisfying and stable catalytic activity towards hydrogen evolution reduction (HER) due to the existed pyrrolic N. Compared with other synthesis methods, our method is more convenient and can be amplified under atmospheric pressure, which showed great practical application significance.

Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Data analysis. Nini Ye: Writing-original draft, Investigation, Validation, Data analysis. Xingqiu Li: Investigation, Validation. Xian Li: Investigation, Validation. Enze Wang: Investigation, Validation.Chenglu Liang:Writing-review & editing, Supervision, Data analysis. Xiangfang Peng: Writing-review & editing, Supervision, data analysis.

Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Nini Ye: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis. Xingqiu Li: Investigation, Validation. Xian Li: Investigation, Validation. Enze Wang: Investigation, Validation. Chenglu Liang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis. Xiangfang Peng: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Talent construction fund of Fujian University of Technology (E0600303 and E0600338).
